visits.text: Visit the Bay of Bones, a reconstruction of a prehistoric stilt village on the water, 20 minutes' drive south of Ohrid. The small museum on site explains Bronze Age life. Quiet in the morning. Entry: 200 denars.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Shirok Sokak, Bitola's pedestrian boulevard, radiates rare energy for a city this size. Neoclassical and Austro-Hungarian façades, overflowing café terraces, former consulates—Bitola was the 'City of Consuls' in Ottoman times. Order an XXL iced coffee and watch the parade: here, the evening promenade is a national sport.

At the end of Shirok Sokak, the Clock Tower (Saat Kula) has dominated the city since the 16th century. It's Bitola's symbol, visible from almost everywhere. The municipal park just beside it is perfect for a shaded break.

Bitola's bazaar is small but authentic: goldsmiths, leatherworkers, tailors still rub shoulders here. Less spectacular than those of Skopje or Ohrid, it has the advantage of being almost tourist-free.

Make a detour to the Haydar Kadi mosque and the Jeni Džamija, two witnesses to the Ottoman period. The Goce Delčev Memorial House pays homage to the national hero of the independence struggle. Modest entry fee.

activities.text: Try pastrmajlija, the 'Macedonian pizza' of Prilep and Bitola: an oval dough covered with sliced dried meat. It's the identity dish of the Pelagonia region. Try it at Giro or one of the restaurants around the park—count on 200–300 denars.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: The Radika Canyon, beside the main road, plunges between cliffs 5 to 20 metres high. Stop at Rostušë village to see the Duf waterfall, spectacular between May and July when flow is at its peak.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: The P504 road crossing Galičica National Park is a ribbon of switchback asphalt between two worlds: Lake Ohrid on one side, Lake Prespa on the other. With 227 km² of protected territory and a summit at 2,254 m (Magaro), the park shelters around ten plant species endemic to this spot alone, found nowhere else on Earth.

activities.text: Hike up to the viewpoint where both lakes appear simultaneously. The trail from the main pass takes about 1hr 30min one way. Bring at least 2 litres of water—no springs en route. The view, on a clear day, is the finest reward of the entire road trip.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Galičnik is a ghost village—except one weekend a year, around 12 July. The Galičnik Wedding Festival, revived in 1963, marries a real couple according to rituals centuries old: procession on horseback, traditional dances, zurla pipe and drum music. The rest of the year, the stone houses sleep beneath the wind.

Climb up to the highest houses in the village to see typical Mijak architecture: dry-stone walls, carved wooden balconies, slate roofs. A handful of families still return in summer. The views over the Bistra mountains are worth the climb.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

activities.text: Take a boat from Stenje or Konjsko to Golem Grad island, nicknamed 'Snake Island'. Roman ruins, medieval churches with frescoes, harmless grass snakes everywhere and not a single tourist. Allow 2hrs on site. Boats only leave when there are enough passengers.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Founded in the 4th century BC by Philip II of Macedon (Alexander the Great's father), Heraclea Lyncestis remained prosperous thanks to its position on the Via Egnatia, the great Roman road from the Adriatic to the Bosphorus. The Roman theatre with 20 tiers hosted gladiatorial combat until the 4th century.

The early Christian basilica mosaics are the highlight: floral motifs, endemic animals, geometric patterns—over a total surface of 1,300 m². They rank among the best-preserved in the Balkans. Allow an unhurried hour, the site covers 4 hectares. Entry: 120 denars.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: The Makedonium rises like a spaceship above Kruševo, the country's highest town (over 1,350 m). This brutalist monument from 1974, designed by Jordan and Iskra Grabuloski, commemorates the 1903 Ilinden Uprising against the Ottoman Empire. Inside, blazing stained glass and the tomb of Nikola Karev, president of the short-lived Republic of Kruševo. It featured on the old 10,000-denar banknotes (series withdrawn from circulation).

Descend into Kruševo's cobbled alleys to admire traditional 19th-century architecture: blue-painted windows, red tiled roofs, wooden galleries. The town was founded by Aromanian shepherds and merchants—this community left a strong imprint.

The Toše Proeski Memorial House, for the beloved singer who died in 2007, is a small pop pilgrimage for all Macedonians. Whether or not you know his music, the emotion of local visitors is enough to make the place touching.

activities.text: Sit in a café in Kruševo's Old Bazaar and order a Turkish coffee. The altitude keeps the air cool even in high summer—you'll hear the hum of conversations from up the hill. Also try local halva at Tagas Balkan Candy Shop.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: After the bustle of Ohrid, Lake Prespa feels like a bath of silence. Shared between three countries (North Macedonia, Albania, Greece), this is apple country—Resen, the nearby town, is the country's orchard. The village of Stenje, at the water's edge, has a new promenade and an almost deserted pebble beach.

Don't miss Slivnica monastery, perched above Lake Prespa, with its well-preserved frescoes. Less known than Ohrid's great monasteries, it offers rare tranquillity and a panorama over the whole lake basin.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: The water is a suspiciously emerald green—and yet Matka Canyon is 100% natural. Well, almost: the 1938 dam on the Treska River created this lake wedged between limestone cliffs. The trail follows the north shore for 7 km, medieval monasteries cling to the rock faces. Arrive before 9am to beat the crowds.

Vrelo Cave, accessible only by boat, shelters an underground lake 212 m deep—among the deepest in Europe. The boat visit takes 20 minutes out, the return offers a stunning view of the canyon. Bring cash: no cards accepted here.

activities.text: Rent a kayak at the main jetty and paddle up the canyon at your own pace. The water is calm, the walls rise sheer, silence is total as soon as you pass the restaurant. Rental: around 500 denars per half hour.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: The half-submerged church of St Nicholas rises from Lake Mavrovo like a ghost. Drowned by the dam's rising waters, it remains standing, photogenic at any hour. The water level varies by season—in summer, you can approach it on foot.

Mavrovo National Park is the country's largest: over 73,000 hectares, three mountain ranges (Šar, Korab, Bistra), and North Macedonia's highest peak, Mount Korab at 2,764 m. The road crossing the park is a spectacle in itself. Download offline maps—signal is temperamental.

activities.text: Explore Sharkova Dupka cave, a ten-minute walk from Mavrovo village. A metal staircase descends into the cavity, lit by spotlights. Short but surprising—especially if you weren't expecting anything from a village cave.

Hike to Lake Mavrovo via the trail starting from the village. Allow 2hrs round trip for an easy route, with views of snow-capped ridges in spring. Bring water and layers: weather changes fast in the mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

places.name: Ohrid

visits.text: The Church of St John at Kaneo, perched on a cliff above the lake, is the postcard image of all North Macedonia. Arrive at sunrise: you'll be alone with the reflections of Lake Ohrid on the 13th-century stone walls. After 10am, there's a queue for a selfie.

Climb the ramparts of Samuel's Fortress, named after the Bulgarian tsar who made it his capital in the 10th century. The view embraces the lake, the old town and the Albanian mountains beyond. Entry: 60 denars—a steal.

Ohrid once had 365 churches—one for every day of the year, hence its nickname 'Jerusalem of the Balkans'. The church of Sveta Bogorodica Perivlepta (1295) houses remarkable Byzantine frescoes. The ancient Roman theatre, still used for concerts, dates from Hellenistic times—the only one of its kind in the country.

Wander the old town on foot: cobbled alleys, Ottoman houses with overhanging balconies, filigree shops. Ohrid's Old Bazaar has existed since the 12th century. Try the lake trout at a terrace restaurant—it's endemic and protected, check it comes from a farm.

The Plaošnik archaeological site, next to the fortress, is where St Clement of Ohrid founded the first Slavic university in the 9th century. The Church of St Clement and St Panteleimon was reconstructed in 2002 on the original foundations. Excavations ongoing—each season brings new discoveries.

activities.text: Take a boat from Ohrid harbour along the coast to Kaneo and St Naum monastery. The crossing takes about an hour, Lake Ohrid reaches depths of 288 m—one of Europe's oldest lakes, around 3 million years old. Book in the morning for the best light.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

map_keywords: Church of St John at Kaneo Ohrid

visits.text: Pelister National Park, the country's oldest (1948), protects Baba Mountain and its 35 summits above 2,000 m. Its star: the Molika pine (Pinus peuce), a rare five-needle species that can live over 230 years. Walking through these forests is like crossing a living museum.

activities.text: Hike up to Big Lake (Golemo Ezero), one of the two 'Eyes of Pelister', nestled at 2,218 m altitude. The trail from Nizhepole village climbs steadily for about 3 hours. A shelter stands by the lake—often closed except summer weekends. Along the way, pick wild blueberries if it's the season.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Marko's Towers (Markovi Kuli) dominate Prilep from a granite hill, visible from across the city. A 14th-century fortress of Serbian King Marko Mrnjavčević, the site has been occupied since the Bronze Age. Allow 30 minutes' climb from Varoš village just below. The view sweeps 270° over the Pelagonia plain.

Before climbing, cross Varoš village, older than Prilep itself. The great 15th-century iron gate of the Archangel Michael monastery is worth seeing. Under the main inn, a deep well—ask them to open it.

Prilep's Tobacco Museum is one of the few in the world dedicated to this subject. The city is the heart of Macedonian tobacco cultivation—the local 'yellow gold'. Even if the topic doesn't fascinate you, the building and ethnographic collections are worth the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Kitsch or genius? The Stone Bridge (Kamen Most) has spanned the Vardar since the 15th century, but it's the massive statues from the 'Skopje 2014' project that steal the show—a triumphal arch, a giant Alexander on his column. Free and visible in five minutes, it's the city's best selfie spot.

Step inside the Mother Teresa Memorial House, just off Macedonia Square. Born here in 1910 as Anjezë Gonxhe Bojaxhiu, the future Nobel laureate grew up on this street. The small museum traces her life with restraint—allow 30 minutes. Free entry.

If you're up for bargaining, the Old Bazaar (Stara Čaršija) is one of the largest Ottoman bazaars in the Balkans. Cobbled lanes, mosques, hammams, the scent of roasted coffee. Prices are low, but haggle anyway—it's expected.

Climb up to the Kale Fortress above the bazaar. The walls date from the 6th century, the view sweeps across Skopje and Mount Vodno. No entry fee, though none of the small museums inside are open to the public yet.

The Museum of the Macedonian Struggle, opened in 2011, traces five centuries of Ottoman rule and the resistance movements that followed. The guided tour takes an hour and costs 300 denars for foreigners. The wax reconstructions are striking—sometimes a little too much so.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a tasting at Château Kamnik, on Skopje's northern outskirts. This estate founded in 2004 cultivates 13 hectares of vineyards and its wines appear on the menu at Michelin-starred Hide in London. Count on 1,500 denars per person for the tour with charcuterie platter.

Take the Mount Vodno cable car up to the Millennium Cross, standing 66 metres tall and visible from across the city. At the top, 360° panorama over Skopje and the surrounding mountains. Avoid weekends: the queue grows fast.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Last stop before Skopje: the archaeological site of Stobi, former capital of the Roman province of Macedonia Secunda. Founded as a Paeonian city, passing under Macedonian then Roman control, the site spreads its basilicas, baths and theatre over several hectares. The peacock mosaic in the baptistery is splendid—it gave the neighbouring winery its emblem.

activities.text: Finish the road trip with a tasting at Stobi Winery, founded in 2009 right next to the ruins. With 500 hectares of vines and the local Vranec grape as star, it's one of the country's most awarded estates (Decanter, Mundus Vini). The view from the terrace embraces the vineyard and the Vardar valley.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: Saint Jovan Bigorski Monastery must be earned: you have to climb a winding road above the Radika River to reach this eagle's nest founded in 1020. The reward is inside—an iconostasis carved in wood between 1829 and 1835 by master craftsman Petre Filipov of Gari, considered one of the finest in the Balkans. Six years of work for a hypnotic result.

According to monastery tradition, an icon of St John the Baptist miraculously appeared here in 1020, then vanished and reappeared after Ottoman destruction in the 16th century. Visitors are also shown what is said to be the saint's forearm. Believer or not, the place commands silence.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: St Naum Monastery, founded in 905, dominates Lake Ohrid from its cliff, 400 metres from the Albanian border. The gilded iconostasis dates from 1711, the carved woodwork on the choir stalls is among the oldest in the country. Peacocks strut through the gardens—improbable but true detail.

Just beside the monastery, artesian springs gush from the mountain's roots with astonishing force. The crystal-clear water feeds directly into Lake Ohrid. Take the small boat that goes up the springs (100 denars)—the bottom is so clear you can see every pebble at 5 metres.

activities.text: Have lunch at the monastery restaurant: the lake trout, grilled with lemon, is said to be the best in the region. Terrace with lake view, attentive service, reasonable prices for the setting.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: You don't expect this in a noisy town: the Šarena Džamija (Painted Mosque) of Tetovo looks like a giant deck of cards set down by the Pena River. Built in the 15th century then rebuilt in the 19th by Abdurrahman Pasha, its façade is a patchwork of floral and geometric frescoes. The interior is even wilder—ask the guardian to open up, he often speaks German.

Fifteen minutes' walk from the mosque, Arabati Baba Tekke is a Bektashi Sufi monastery founded in 1538, during the reign of Suleiman the Magnificent. The walled complex was disputed between religious communities for decades. Today, the residents welcome you with tea and an improvised tour. Keep clothing modest.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop

visits.text: If you have the energy, the hiking trail between Marko's Towers and Treskavec Monastery (7 km, about 3hrs one way) is one of the country's finest. The 12th-century monastery, beneath Zlatovrv peak (1,262 m), possesses exceptional Byzantine frescoes. Only one monk still lives there. Trail marked red and white, also accessible by car.

days_plan.itin_title: North Macedonia: 10-day lakes and monasteries loop
